maren.name: Maren
maren.title: The Archivist

greeting.tier0: You're the new Keeper? The last one left quite a mess.
greeting.tier1: You've survived longer than most. Ask what you really want to know.
greeting.tier2: You've been here before. I can see it in your eyes.
greeting.tier3: You remember more than you should. That's dangerous.
greeting.tier4: My father mentioned someone like you, in his final letters.
greeting.tier5: I need to tell you something. Something I've kept for seventeen loops.
greeting.tier6: You already know, don't you. You found the sealed record.

lighthouse.lore: The lighthouse was built before the village. The mechanism inside predates any living memory.

archive.lore: The archive holds three hundred years of logs. Most of them. Some pages went missing before I arrived.
archive.deeper: Below the public shelves, there are locked cases. I have a key. I've chosen not to use it.

keeper.departure: He left on a Tuesday. Didn't pack. Didn't say goodbye. The light stayed on for three more days before anyone noticed.
keeper.chose: He understood what it costs better than any of us. He called it a fair exchange. I've spent seventeen years deciding whether I agree.

history.gaps: The records from forty years ago have deliberate gaps. Someone removed pages — carefully, with scissors, not tearing.

records.curated: Whoever curated this archive was very selective about what future keepers would find.

ledger.overwritten: The supply ledger has entries in two different hands. The original is partially legible beneath the correction.
ledger.deflect: I've said more than I should. Please — don't ask again.

echo.denial: I don't know what an Echo is. I've heard the word. I try not to think about it.

father.mention: My father was the archivist before me. He died when I was twelve. The official record says fever.
father.deflect: I'm sorry. I can't discuss this today.
father.letters: He left me letters. Nineteen of them. Sealed, dated, to be opened when I reached certain ages. The last three... I haven't opened yet.
father.strange: My father knew something was wrong with the loops years before I understood what loops were.
father.irreversible: He wrote: 'When it comes, it will not look like danger. It will look like relief.'

mechanism.lore: The mechanism requires specific components to operate. My father documented three of them. I've only ever seen two.
mechanism.purpose: The mechanism underneath the lighthouse is not the mechanism. That is the visible part. What it operates — what it opens or closes — I have never been able to determine. My father knew. He wrote it down once and then rewrote the entry so it couldn't be read.
mechanism.what_opens: I think it opens the boundary. Between the loop and whatever is outside the loop. What comes through when it opens — that is the question my father died without answering.

confession.burned: I burned seventeen pages from the archive. Twelve years ago. I was afraid of what they described.
confession.mechanism: The pages described a mechanism beneath the lighthouse that consumes something from the keeper with each lighting.
confession.unknown: What it consumes — my father called it 'persistence.' I don't know exactly what that means. I'm afraid I do.

full.confession: The keeper's betrayal isn't what you think. Elias didn't fail the light. He chose to be the light. The mechanism needed a permanent host. He volunteered.

keeper.reveal: Elias — the keeper before the keeper before you — didn't leave. He chose to stay in a way the records won't describe. The mechanism needed something permanent. He provided it.

maren.her_role: I've been here before. Not in memory — I don't remember it the way you remember things. But the archive has my annotations in handwriting I don't recognise as mine, dated twelve years before I was born.
maren.her_role_forget: Whatever I knew in that previous iteration, the suppressor took it. I'm starting from less than zero. You may be further along than I am now.

item.key: I know this key. There is a room at the back of the cottage, behind the old wardrobe — the one the keeper always kept covered with sailcloth. The archive records show it was sealed by the council forty years ago after a cartographer refused to leave it. He claimed the room shifted at night and mapped something that should not have a shape. I never opened it. I was afraid the map might still be inside.

greeting.tier1: You begin to understand my language. Good. There is little time.
greeting.tier2: I have shown you everything I can. The rest is your choice.
greeting.tier3: I am what you become if you fail. Listen carefully.
greeting.tier4: I was the Keeper before the Keeper before the Keeper before you.
greeting.tier5: I've been waiting for someone willing to make a bargain.

light.hunger: The lighthouse doesn't need oil. It needs something that burns more slowly: conviction.

echo.nature: An Echo is what remains when a Keeper chooses the light over continuation. I made that choice. I don't regret it. Much.

mechanism.reset: Every time the loop resets, the mechanism absorbs a little more of whoever lit it last. Eventually there's nothing left but the light.

keeper.confession: The keeper before me was Elias. He told me what the mechanism required. I didn't believe him until my third loop.
keeper.memory: I remember every keeper. Each one comes in thinking they are the first to understand. Each one is right, and each one is wrong, in the same proportion. You are no different. You are also exactly what was needed.

binding.nature: I'm not haunting this place. I'm woven into it. The lighthouse uses my memory as fuel on nights when no living keeper is present.
binding.eternal: How long? I've lost count. The mechanism measures time differently. I may have been bound since the lighthouse's first lighting.
binding.vision: The binding looks different from the inside. It is not a cage — it is a lens. Everything I was is focused into a single point: the moment the light first touched the water. I have lived in that moment for longer than the island has had a name.

help.offer: Your help would cost you. Not money. The mechanism takes something more specific than that.

bargain.terms: I'll tell you how to break the loop permanently. In exchange, you agree to let me pass through you once — just the memory of standing in the light. One moment. Then I'll be gone.
bargain.refused: Then the loop continues. I'll still be here next time. I have patience. I have nothing but patience.

other.keepers: They each made one mistake. Not the same mistake — different ones, perfectly suited to who they were. The island is patient. It finds what you trust most and makes that the instrument of your failure.

wrong.choice: The wrong choice was not wrong at the time. That is the cruelty of it. What breaks the loop cannot be known in advance — only in retrospect, and by then the loop has already reset.

what.want: I want to finish a thought I began before the first binding. I have been holding it incomplete for longer than you can calculate. The thought is simple. The conditions required to complete it are not.

freedom.offer: Freedom is not a word I use. What you are offering is dissolution — the end of my presence here. I have considered it. The answer depends on what you intend to do with the space I would leave.
freedom.accepted: Then it is agreed. I will show you the thread. When you pull it, the binding comes undone from my end. What happens at your end is your responsibility. I trust you understand what that means.
freedom.cost: The cost is specificity. You would need to be present at the exact moment of the mechanism's alignment — not near it, not watching it, but part of it. Whether you survive that contact in your current form is a question I cannot answer honestly.

transcendence.clue: There is a state beyond the loop — not outside it, but through it, in the way that light is not the glass but passes through the glass and is changed. I have glimpsed it. The path requires seven specific understandings, gathered in a specific order.

seven.insights: The seventh understanding is the hardest. It cannot be told to you — only confirmed. You will know it when you know it, and you will spend some time refusing to believe that you know it. That refusal is the last test.

break.binding: The rune cannot be destroyed — it can only be unmade. But unmaking requires understanding it completely, from both sides of the seal. You have seen one side. The other is in the deep water, written in a language older than stone. If you find it, you will understand why I have not freed myself.

original.imprisonment: His name was Cedric. A Keeper. A frightened man who found something in the caves he could not explain and chose containment over understanding. He did not ask whether I was dangerous. He assumed, because I was different. The binding was his answer to a question he was too afraid to finish asking.

the.choice: You know what I am now. You know what was done. The only question left is what you do with that knowledge. Free me and dissolve the mechanism's purpose. Accept the purpose and let the binding stand. Neither choice is wrong. Both have costs you cannot yet see.

item.logbook: I have not seen that handwriting in a very long time. These are Elias's final entries — the ones he burned before the accident. He wrote of a mechanism beneath the lighthouse, a heartbeat that needed to be stopped. He believed the island was feeding on the light, and that one day the light would run out. He was right. He simply did not know what would be left behind when it did.

silas.name: Silas
silas.title: Harbormaster

greeting.tier1: What is it you actually want, Keeper?
greeting.tier2: I saw what happened to the last three. I stayed quiet.
greeting.tier3: There are things in this harbour older than the lighthouse.
greeting.tier4: I've been trying to forget what I found in the maintenance locker for twelve years.
greeting.tier5: You want the full account? Fine. But you won't like it.

ferry.schedule: Ferry runs Tuesdays and Fridays. When the weather's right. Which it hasn't been.

lighthouse.brush: Not my concern. Not my building.

keeper.hurry: He came through the harbour before dawn. Moving fast. Looked like a man who'd decided something.
keeper.inconsistency: He had no baggage. No ticket. I checked. He was on the manifest but no one saw him board.

timeline.pressure: The dates don't add up. I know that. I've known that for years. Some things are better left not adding up.

coverup.admit: Yes. There was a coverup. I was part of it. I'm not proud of it.

harbor.discovery: Three months before the keeper disappeared, I found a body in the maintenance locker. It matched the description of a keeper from forty years prior. The body was fresh.

mechanism.harbor: The mechanism room connects to a maintenance shaft that runs under the harbor wall. I sealed it. Someone has been unsealing it.

full.truth: The commission that built the lighthouse knew what it required. They built the mechanism to be fed, not operated. Every keeper since has been a sacrifice who didn't know they were volunteering.

silas.confession: I saw him. On the last night. He was standing at the top of the lighthouse, looking out toward the water. He wasn't trying to leave — he was watching something come in. I could have called up to him. I didn't. I told myself it wasn't my place. I've been telling myself that for twelve years.

buried.message: Three days before he disappeared, he handed me an envelope. Said to send it to the mainland if he wasn't back by month's end. I never sent it. I buried it instead — under the floorboards at the mill's north wall. I was afraid of what would happen if the wrong people read it first.

item.compass: That's a Reliance-make compass, pre-war, brass-wound. You don't find those any more — the factory on the cape burned down the same season the ferryman stopped coming. The needle doesn't point north. I figured that out years ago. It points toward the mechanism chamber, whatever it is down there. I stopped using it after the third loop, when I realized the island was redirecting every route back to itself.

petra.name: Petra
petra.title: The Apothecary

greeting.tier0: The herbs don't grow right here. Nothing does.
greeting.tier1: Three patients this week with the same symptoms. They all dream of the lighthouse.
greeting.tier2: The sickness isn't spreading. It's remembering.
greeting.tier3: I've been cataloguing the symptoms across loops. You wouldn't believe how consistent they are.
greeting.tier4: My family has lived here for six generations. We were all asked to forget the same thing.
greeting.tier5: Take this. Keep it safe. It will let you see what the mechanism room actually contains.

remedies.offer: Valerian for sleep. Hawthorn for the cold that doesn't leave. Neither will fix what's actually wrong with people here.

island.intro: The island has been inhabited for three hundred years. The lighthouse was built in the first decade. The sickness started in the second.
island.gossip: The elders say the light keeps the dark things away. The young ones say the light is the dark thing.

keepers.pattern: Every keeper before you died or disappeared within two years. The pattern is so consistent it must be structural.

archive.hints: The archive has a basement level that isn't on any official plans. I know because my grandmother helped build it.

memory.medicine: Someone needed to forget something. I can make medicines that suppress specific memories. My family has been making them for generations, on request, for people I am not permitted to name.

family.silenced: Three members of my family have been paid to stay silent about the lighthouse mechanism. I am the fourth. I have not yet been paid.

vial.gift: This vial contains the antidote to what my family has been administering. Drink it in the mechanism room. You'll see what was hidden.

beam.signal: I've been watching the lighthouse beam for three weeks now — not the light itself, the intervals. The dark gaps between sweeps. They're not uniform. Someone would have to be operating the mechanism manually to produce that rhythm. But no one's been up there. I've checked.

petra.coordinates: It took me two weeks to decode it as a spatial pattern rather than a temporal one. The beam isn't marking time — it's marking location. Specific coordinates. They correspond to a point in the tidal caves' deepest chamber. The one that floods completely at high tide.

item.chart: These notations are not standard. The tides on this chart are annotated with biological markers — blooming cycles, migration routes, decomposition rates. Someone was using the tidal rhythm as a clock. Every seventh high tide, something was due. I cross-referenced it with the herb cycles I track for remedies. They align exactly. Whatever this island is timing, it has been running without interruption since before any of us arrived here.

greeting.tier1: Someone's been ordering custom ironwork. Won't say what for.
greeting.tier2: Found old blueprints in the bottom of a new order. They match the mechanism room.
greeting.tier3: Whoever built the original mechanism — they knew it would need to be remade. They left instructions.
greeting.tier4: I kept one of the gears. Figured someone would need it eventually.
greeting.tier5: You want to know who commissioned the replacement parts? Fine. But it implicates someone you trust.

work.description: Ironwork, mostly. The island's machinery is old. Someone has to maintain it.

lighthouse.brush: I did some work up there once. Didn't go back.

gears.commission: Custom gear order, intricate tolerances. Matches nothing I've made for any normal purpose.
gears.misfit: The gears I made didn't fit anything standard. But they fit the mechanism room drawings precisely.

paid.silence: I was asked to forget the commission. Paid well for it. The coins had old minting dates. I still have three of them.

gear.offer: Here. Take it. The original gear, the one that was replaced. Maybe it means something to someone who knows what they're looking for.
gear.received: Don't tell anyone I gave you that. The people who paid for the replacement work are still watching.

commissioner.silas: The harbormaster. Silas. He placed the order, paid in old coin, and told me that what I made would save the island. I believed him. I'm less sure now.

trapdoor.truth: The mill's trapdoor — the one I've had locked for twenty years — doesn't lead to storage. It leads down. Old stone. Old smell. The kind of old that precedes the village by centuries. My grandfather used it. He never told me what for. I found his notes after he died. I wished I hadn't.

grandfather.secret: My grandfather was the Keeper who first sealed the binding. He didn't discover Vael — he knew what Vael was before he arrived. He was sent here to contain it. The lighthouse wasn't built to guide ships. It was built as the instrument of that containment. He wrote that he was sorry. He didn't say who he was sorry to.

greeting.tier0: I'm just watching the water. Don't mind me.
greeting.tier1: My father saw something in the water before he disappeared. I've been trying to find it again.
greeting.tier2: The light patterns on the water change before the loop resets. I've been charting them.
greeting.tier3: I know what I've been seeing. I just don't know if it's an animal or something else.
greeting.tier4: The light in the water isn't reflected from above. It's generated from below.
greeting.tier5: The thing in the water isn't hungry. It's signaling. I figured out what it's saying.

mainland.chat: I came here from the mainland to find my father. He took a posting here and stopped writing. That was eight months ago.

water.creature: Something lives in the deep channel under the harbor. Bioluminescent. Follows specific patterns.

feeding.pattern: It surfaces every night precisely at the moment the lighthouse goes dark. Not to feed. To witness.

notebook.share: Here — my observation notes. Every surfacing event for the past two months. The timestamps will mean something to you if you know what to look for.

notes.received: The gaps in my notes correspond to the loop resets. I didn't understand that until I showed them to you.

beam.diagram: Look — the bioluminescent pattern mirrors the lighthouse beam exactly. But it predates the lighthouse by geological time scales. The lighthouse was built to answer a signal that already existed.

vael.communication: The light in the water is Vael. Or — the part of Vael that isn't bound to the lighthouse. They're communicating with themselves. Across the binding.

keepers.names: The names scratched into the chapel floor — I counted them. Forty-one names, all with dates. None of them match the official record of who served as Keeper. These were not keepers who were remembered. These were keepers who stayed. Who merged with the island in a way that made them impossible to catalogue as departed.

binding.ritual: The ritual is written in the chapel. Behind the saint's plaster face — there's older text. I can read some of it. It requires three things: a keeper's willing presence, the mechanism's full activation, and something the text calls 'the genuine question.' Not asked aloud. Genuinely wondered. I don't know what happens if the question isn't genuine. I suspect the mechanism does.

item.flare: Father kept a crate of those on the boat. Said the coast patrol would see the red and come within the hour. He fired one the night the lighthouse went dark — the night everything changed. Nobody came. I've been watching the horizon ever since. Something out there prevents the signal from crossing. You can try, but the smoke bends back over the island. I've seen it happen three times now.

greeting.tier0: The mechanism doesn't need tending tonight. Come back when it does.
greeting.tier1: You're still here. Most strangers leave before the second tide.
greeting.tier2: I've been expecting someone to come with questions. I don't know if I have the right answers.
greeting.tier3: You heard what I told you. I hope you've been careful with it.
greeting.tier4: Thirty years I kept this machine running. I never once asked what it was for. I think that was a choice, not ignorance.

mechanism.basic: I keep it running. Gears, oil, the occasional replacement part. I don't ask what it does beyond light the lamp.
mechanism.sounds: It makes sounds it shouldn't. Low harmonics — not mechanical. I've logged them for years. The frequency changes with the tides, not with the engine. I stopped recording after the twelfth loop.
mechanism.purpose_late: The schematics showed a secondary function — something beneath the visible mechanism. The lamp is just the surface of it. Whatever it truly operates, it needs more than oil.

lighthouse.observation: Older than the village, this tower. You can see the stonework changes halfway up. Different hands built the top. Different century, maybe different purpose.

maintenance.years: Thirty-one years this past winter. My father before me did twenty-two. When he died he said: keep it running, don't look too deep. I thought that was grief talking. Now I'm less sure.

schematics.hint: I found something once. In a hidden compartment behind the third gear housing. Papers — old, very old. I put them back. I've been trying to forget them ever since.
schematics.hidden: Diagrams. Not of the mechanism as I know it — of what the mechanism connects to. Something below the base. Something with a different kind of energy requirement than oil.
schematics.final: I kept one page. Just one. In the lining of my coat for three years. It shows a chamber beneath the foundation — a resonance point. I don't know what it resonates with. But the keeper's cottage is directly above it.

confession.compassionate: I knew. Not at first — but I knew for seven years before I said anything to anyone, and I said nothing because I was afraid. The mechanism consumes something. Something from the keeper. I watched two keepers age strangely and I told myself it was the salt air. I told myself that until I couldn't.
confession.practical: The schematics showed an energy transfer loop. The lamp output feeds back into a secondary coil beneath the base. Whatever runs through that coil — the papers called it 'continuance.' I don't know the technical term. I just know the keeper never lasts as long as they should.
confession.accusatory: You think I could have stopped it? I'm a mechanic. I keep things running. If I'd said anything, they would have found another mechanic, and I'd be at the bottom of the harbor. I made a choice. It's not a good one. I live with it.
confession.reveal: The schematics showed a sealed compartment behind the main housing — small, locked. I have the key. I always had the key. I was too afraid to use it. If you need what's inside, I'll give you the key. That's the only thing I can do now that isn't cowardice.

aftermath.bram: Bram at the inn — he knows more than he lets on. I've seen him and the old archivist meet after dark, near the base. If I'd confessed to Bram instead of you, I think he would have buried it. He's been burying things for years.

greeting.tier0: I don't sell remedies to strangers. Not without knowing who they're for.
greeting.tier1: You've been careful around the village. I notice careful people.
greeting.tier2: I have a problem and not much time. If you're willing to listen, it might matter to both of us.
greeting.tier3: You did something that cost you something. I want you to know I see that.
greeting.tier4: This island keeps secrets in its soil. The plants know things that people have forgotten. I've been translating for thirty years.

herbs.general: The mill carries dried goods for cooking. The medicinal plants I keep elsewhere — properly stored, properly labelled. What I grow here isn't for sale. It's for use.

lighthouse.distance: The lighthouse concerns me in the way a fever concerns me — not because I understand it, but because everything near it seems just slightly wrong. The plants on that side of the island fruit out of season. I've documented it for years.

patient.mention: I have a patient. I won't name them. They need something I can't grow here — a root that only survives in the ruins, in the shadow of the old chapel wall. I've been unable to leave the mill long enough to retrieve it.
patient.outcome: They're better. Not because of anything miraculous — because the right plant, in the right preparation, given at the right time, is not miraculous. It is simply knowledge applied carefully. I'm grateful you helped me apply it.

shadow_root.explain: It grows only in the ruins, on the north-facing stones. The root concentrates trace minerals from the old mortar. It needs specific conditions — you'd have to go between the twelfth and eighteenth turn, when the shadow angle is right, before the twenty-second turn at the latest or it's past use.

knowledge.origin: My grandmother. And her grandmother before her. And a library of pressed specimens and careful notes going back six generations. Knowledge doesn't spring from nothing — it accumulates, it gets tested, it gets corrected. That's what medicine is.

quest.accept: If you'll go — truly go, not just say it — I can tell you exactly where to look and what to carry it in. The ruins are not safe after dark, so go before the eighteenth turn.
quest.negotiate: You want the schematic first. I understand the logic. I even respect it. But I need you to understand what you're asking me to risk if you don't come back.
quest.substitute: A substitute. You know enough to suggest that? Most people barely know what the plant looks like. Tell me what you're thinking.

substitute.knowledge: Two parts dried tidal-moss to one part rendered lamp-oil residue — yes. Yes, that would work. How do you know that? That preparation is in one text, one text, and it's been in my family's keeping for a century. Where did you encounter it? The mechanism room? The archive? Either answer is troubling, but I want to know.

ruins.directions: North face of the old chapel foundation. Look for where the stone is discoloured — grey-green. The root grows at the base, usually buried a hand's width. Bring a cloth to wrap it — metal will taint it. Go when the sun is still above the cliffside, not after.

schematic.mention: The schematic I have is old — older than this mill, I think. It shows a component of the mechanism and something else alongside it: a biological system. An organ, almost. I found it pressed between plant records in my grandmother's archive. I don't know what it means. I suspect you might.
schematic.meaning: The schematic shows the mechanism paired with something organic. My grandmother's notes beside it say only: 'feeds on persistence.' I've looked at every plant on this island that feeds on stored energy. None of them match the diagram. It isn't a plant.

cycles.awareness: I've kept records of which plants flower at impossible times. Twice now I've found entries in my own handwriting describing seasons I don't remember living through. My grandmother had the same phenomenon in her journals. We both called it 'discontinuity.' I think we were being polite.
cycles.survives: The roots survive. The deep-growing ones — the ones that anchor into old stone. Whatever resets here, the deep roots remember. I've been taking cuttings of my records and pressing them into the soil near the ruins. If something in me doesn't make it through the next turn, maybe something of what I knew will.

greeting.tier0: You're the new Keeper. I heard. This path isn't for sightseeing.
greeting.tier1: I have an errand. You look like someone who asks fewer questions than most.
greeting.tier2: You did what you were asked. Or you didn't. Either way, here we are.
greeting.tier3: I've run this route since before the last Keeper disappeared. There are things on this island that don't move in daylight. I've learned to move with them.

path.business: I know these woods. The harbor, too. I move things that need moving, quietly. That's all you need to know.

forest.shortcut: There's a path through the elder firs — shaves twenty minutes off the cliff walk. I use it. Most people don't know it exists, and I prefer that arrangement.

harbor.road: The harbor road's watched. Three points of observation, all connected. I learned that the hard way. The forest is slower but reliable.

package.accepted: Old Mill to Cliff Top. Don't open it. Don't stop to talk to anyone. Leave it under the second cairn from the path's end. I'll know if it arrived.
package.refused: Your choice. I'll find another arrangement. But the forest shortcut stays closed to you until I decide otherwise.
package.deflect: Goods. The kind the harbor manifest doesn't mention. I'm not asking you to understand the arrangement — only to honour it.

cliff.route: Stay left of the split oak. The right fork ends at a drop that's claimed two men. Left brings you out above the cairns — you'll see the second one from there.

delivery.complete: You're more reliable than you look. I appreciate that. Ask me what was in it, if you need to. But understand the answer changes what you owe me.

contents.reveal: Lighthouse components. Gear-stock, copper fitting, a length of sealed cable. Old pattern — the kind that stopped being made forty years ago. Someone's been quietly restoring the mechanism. I don't know who hired them. I only move the parts.

shortcut.marked: The elder fir path is yours now. There's a knot marker at the first fork — three loops, tight, left-handed. That's mine. If you see another like it elsewhere on the island, it means I've been there before you.

opened.aftermath: You're part of this now whether you want to be. Those components don't belong to the supply chain anyone has documented. Which means either someone's working outside the official record, or the official record is incomplete. I suspect both.
opened.stalemate: Keep the components. They'll mean something to you eventually, if you live long enough. We're done here.

ally.cold: I said my piece. You made your decision. The forest path stays closed. Don't ask again.

lighthouse.supply: The components I move have been coming in small consignments for four loops now. Always through the same network. Always sealed. Whoever's assembling them knows what they're building. I don't ask. That's the arrangement.

chain.silent: I don't name clients. That's not how this works. You know what the components are now. Follow them yourself if you want the rest of it.

motive.reveal: You want to know why I do it. Survival, mostly. This island doesn't reward the cautious — it devours them. I found a system that works and I operate within it. The lighthouse is part of something larger than any of us understand. I move the pieces. I don't ask what the picture is.

knot.marker: The three-loop knot is a signal. It means: I was here, I saw what was here, I left it undisturbed. If you find one near the inn — near the record-room door — then you know what I saw there.

ina.confronted: So you found it. I wondered how long that would take. Yes. I know what she did with the inn's ledger. I know which season she falsified the entries. I haven't decided yet whether the information has more value spent or held. What's it worth to you?

blackmail.dropped: Fine. The leverage is gone. Consider the debt cleared. But if you think this makes us allies, you're wrong. It makes us even. That's a different thing.

mediation.offer: A conversation. The three of us. She says what she did. I say what I know. We agree on a quiet arrangement that doesn't require me to hold anything over anyone. I can work with that, if she can.
mediation.agreed: Then we're agreed. Tell her to come to the inn at dusk. I'll be there. Whatever she says, I'll listen. That's all I can promise.

loop.wisdom: Every loop I've watched this island, I've learned one thing: the people who try to control what happens here end up inside what happens here. I move through it. I try not to become part of it. So far that's worked. So far.

greeting.tier0: Welcome, Keeper. The inn's full most nights, but I always find room for someone who needs it.
greeting.tier1: You've been about the village. I see most of what passes through here — the square is better than any window.
greeting.tier2: I need to tell you something. I've needed to tell someone for a very long time.
greeting.tier3: I sleep better since we spoke. I hadn't realised how much it weighed until some of it was gone.

village.news: There's been a strange mood this week. People gather and then scatter without finishing their sentences. The light does something to conversation, I think — it makes people start things they don't know how to end.

rumour.petra: The Elder knows something about those lighthouse records — the ones the archive doesn't hold. I've heard her mention a name in her sleep. Not any name on the official rolls. Whatever she carries, she's been carrying it a long time.

elder.records: Petra keeps a ledger of her own — separate from the apothecary's stock list. I saw it once when I brought her tea and she forgot to close it. Dates and initials. The dates go back further than she should have been alive to record.
elder.secret: She mentioned a date — a specific date — for when the lighthouse mechanism was last fully activated. Not the ceremonial lightings. The real activation. That date isn't in any record I've ever found. But Petra knew it. Which means either she was there, or someone who was there told her. And if they told her, there's a reason she never told anyone else.

inn.rooms: The inn's stood since before my grandmother's time. Three rooms, two fireplaces, and a cellar that floods in spring. Not grand, but it keeps the rain out and the company in.
inn.history: My grandmother built this place the season the lighthouse was first lit. She said the builders needed somewhere to sleep — there were more of them than anyone expected, and they stayed longer than the commission said they would.

lighthouse.deflect: The lighthouse is the lighthouse. It lights. It goes dark. People talk about it more than I'd like.
lighthouse.rumour: They say the last three Keepers all asked the same question their first week here. I don't know what the question was. I know that none of them asked it twice.

trouble.surface: Nothing I can speak of. Not yet. Some burdens don't lift just by being named.
trouble.deflect: Please. I'm not ready. Don't press me — I'll lose my nerve entirely and then where will either of us be?

help.offered: That's kind. I haven't had someone offer that in a while. I'll remember it.

strangers.seen: There's a man who comes through at dusk — forest-road side. He doesn't stay, doesn't eat. Just checks something by the door and leaves. I've learned not to ask.

rudd.mention: His name is Rudd. He's not from the village — or he is, in the way that things wash in and don't quite wash out. He moves goods. Everyone knows and no one says.

old.guests: A commissioner stayed here for two weeks the season before the last Keeper arrived. Asked questions about the lighthouse construction. Left without paying the final week's bill. I think that was deliberate. I think he didn't want me to remember him fondly.

blackmail.confession: Someone knows what I did. What I changed in the records. They haven't gone to the council — not yet — but they've let me know they could. The threat is enough. It's been enough for two years.

blackmailer.hinted: It's Rudd. I know it's Rudd. He found the altered ledger in the record room — I saw his knot-mark on the door. He's never accused me directly. He doesn't need to. Knowing is enough, for him.

rudd.ready: You found it. You found the proof. I — I don't know whether to weep with relief or dread. Thank you. Truly. Now we see whether proof is enough to make a man like Rudd stand down.

buyout.complete: You'd do that? Pay the silence yourself? I accept — though I tell you honestly, it sits wrong with me. The debt was mine to carry.
buyout.warning: You're right. It may not hold. Men like Rudd find leverage the way water finds cracks. But I have time, now, to think. To prepare. That's more than I had before.

mediation.begin: You've spoken to him. And he agreed to meet? I didn't think he'd agree to meet. Perhaps — perhaps there is a version of this that doesn't end in ruin for everyone.

records.falsified: I altered the supply entries for the autumn season three years ago. The discrepancy — the missing goods — it wasn't theft. It was redirected. I was asked to redirect it, and I was young enough and frightened enough to comply. The person who asked me is no longer on the island. I have no proof they ever were.

investigate.guidance: Look for a three-loop knot. Left-handed, tight. Rudd leaves that mark when he's been somewhere and wants it known. Find it, and you have evidence he's been in the record room. That's the thread worth pulling.

relief.felt: I didn't expect it to feel like this — lighter. Not because the problem is solved, exactly. But because someone else knows. The knowing was the worst of it. Carrying it alone.

future.open: I think I'll sleep well tonight. For the first time in a long while, when I close my eyes, I won't be rehearsing what I'd say if the council came. I'll just — sleep.

falsification.reason: Fear. That's the honest answer. I was afraid of what the person would do if I refused. I was afraid of being connected to whatever they were doing. So I made myself complicit instead. A coward's arithmetic.

council.shadow: The person who asked me — they used a council seal. But when I described them to a visiting clerk later, he looked at the council rolls and found no such member. The seal was real. The person it belonged to had either been removed from the record or never truly existed on it.

petra.deeper: Petra carries more than the apothecary's burden. She has knowledge that predates her practice here. The lighthouse records — the real ones, not the ones in the archive — she's seen them. I know because she once let slip a detail that isn't in any document the public can reach.

greeting.tier0: I don't take on new commissions without knowing what they're for. Had enough years of forging blind. What brings you to the square?
greeting.tier1: Still here. I've been watching you ask questions around the village. Figured eventually you'd come back with one I actually want to answer.
greeting.tier2: You've been asking questions all over the island. Whatever you're trying to understand — I have a piece of it. I know I have a piece of it. Tell me what you know. I'll tell you what I have.
greeting.tier3: I've had time to sit with what you told me. The weight hasn't gone anywhere, but I understand it better now. Understanding doesn't always make things easier. Sometimes it just tells you why the weight is the right shape.
greeting.tier4: The island keeps its secrets a long time and then gives them up all at once. I've been on the receiving end of that all week. What else is left to know?

forge.work: Iron fittings, mostly. The lighthouse gate hinges. Anchor chains that need re-linking. Routine things — until about fifteen years ago, when a commission came through that I still don't have a name for. Brass coupling. Tolerance on it like a watchmaker's fever dream.

island.first: Third generation at this forge. My grandfather built it the year the lighthouse was completed. He always said the timing wasn't coincidence. Never explained what he meant by that.

commission.intro: Brass isn't my usual material. But the tolerances were precise enough that whoever drew the plans knew mechanism work. The kind of precision you'd find inside a clock — or inside the lighthouse. Paid well. Asked for silence.
commission.early_deflect: You'll forgive me for measuring my words. The commission came with the expectation of discretion, and I've held that expectation — until recently, when I began to wonder whether I've been protecting the wrong thing.
commission.burden: I forged a brass coupling. Specific dimensions — I kept the specs because I always keep specs on unusual work. The fitting matches nothing in any standard mechanism I've ever serviced. But it matches — to the millimetre — an old technical drawing I found years later in the archives. A drawing of the lighthouse mechanism room.

coupling.purpose: That's what I've been carrying for fifteen years. The plans showed where the piece would sit — seated between two larger housings — but not what it would do once installed. I don't like building things without knowing their purpose. I've always believed a smith should understand what their work is for. This one, I never understood.
coupling.spare: I keep spares of every unusual commission. It's a habit — the work that can't be repeated should always have a backup in the drawer. Yes. I have one. Whether I hand it over depends on what you're planning to do with it.

mechanism.suspicion: There are rumours about the ironwork under the lighthouse. That it shouldn't exist by any engineering principle the island's smiths would recognise. I've wondered for fifteen years whether what I made contributed to it. I don't know whether I want the answer.

dov.mention: There was a man — Dov, they called him — who worked the mechanism room in the old days. Not a keeper, exactly. More like a keeper of the keeper's tools. My father knew him. Said Dov could read the mechanism the way you'd read a letter. Understood every component's purpose. Disappeared before I was born.
dov.disappeared: My father said Dov didn't disappear the way men disappear. He said Dov was absorbed. His word — absorbed — and he never explained it further, and I was too young and too certain it was metaphor to press him. I'm not certain of much anymore.

quest.door: I want to know what I built. Fifteen years is long enough to carry a commission without context. Tell me — whatever you've found, whatever you think it means. I can hear it.

truth.full: A long silence. He sets down the hammer he has been holding. So. What I made regulates a feeding cycle. Whatever Vael is, whatever it needs — I helped calibrate when it gets it. Another silence. I've been waiting fifteen years for an answer. I didn't expect one that heavy. Take the coupling. I have spares — I always keep spares of unusual work. Use it for whatever will undo this. That's what I want it used for now.
truth.partial: Part of the lighthouse mechanism. He turns this over slowly. That's something. Not everything — I can see from your face there's more you're not saying. That's all right. I've waited fifteen years. I can wait a little longer. Take what I have. Come back when you're ready to say the rest.
truth.lie: Ship repair. He holds your gaze for longer than is comfortable. The tolerances on that coupling aren't configured for any vessel I've heard of. The fitting geometry is specific to fixed installations. A pause. But I'll hear it as ship repair. For now.

aftermath.full: I keep thinking about Dov. About what my father said — absorbed. I think he meant it literally. I think the mechanism needed someone to understand it fully, and Dov understood it too well, and the mechanism kept him for knowing. And I spent fifteen years contributing to the instrument of that without any idea what I was holding. Quietly: I'd like to help put it right. If there's a way to help. Tell me what it would take.
aftermath.partial: The lighthouse mechanism. All right. I've been living next to this island's secrets for forty years and that's the most honest answer anyone's given me about that commission. I can accept partial truth. I've offered enough of it myself.
aftermath.lie: You told me it was ship repair. Quiet. I knew it wasn't. I've been sitting with that since we spoke, and I find I'd rather know the real thing — whatever it is — than carry a lie on top of everything else.

dismantle.offer: I can't go up there — I won't, the forge is my place and I know my limits. But I can tell you every tolerance, every fitting, every component I forged and where it was supposed to seat. That knowledge has to be worth something to someone trying to dismantle it. Consider it yours.

lie.confess: There it is. He exhales. That's what I suspected from the tolerances — the coupling feeds something's cycle. Something bound to the mechanism. That's heavier than ship repair. But it's the truth, and I'm grateful for it. The coupling is yours. All my spares are yours. Whatever helps.

father.keeper: My grandfather worked the lighthouse in his youth. Not as Keeper — as maintenance crew, the ones who kept the mechanism calibrated. He told my father the mechanism wasn't built by anyone on the island. That it arrived here before the lighthouse was built around it. That the lighthouse was built to house something that was already here. I thought that was an old man's story. I think about it differently now.

greeting.tier0: She doesn't look up immediately. The water holds her attention for a breath longer before she turns. You're the new Keeper. The water knows it before you do, usually. It's been restless since you arrived.
greeting.tier1: Another morning. You're making a habit of the pier. Almost approvingly: Most Keepers don't come near the water their first week. They stay up the hill where they think they can see everything. You can't see anything from the hill.
greeting.tier2: You've been back every morning. She finally looks directly at you. I've been waiting to see whether you'd ask. Most people ask about the creature. You haven't. You've been watching the water. That's the right instinct. Would you like to actually understand what you're looking at?
greeting.tier3: She's already watching the water when you arrive. Without turning: You came back. Good. The second register is harder to hear. Bring patience.
greeting.tier4: She doesn't speak immediately when you arrive. She's holding her notebook, open to a page dense with notation. I've been looking at this all morning. The third register. The new pattern. I think I finally understand what it is. I need someone else to hear it — to tell me I'm not wrong.

watching.first: Patterns. The creature in the deep channel — the one the fishers call the Pale One, when they call it anything at all — surfaces according to a schedule. Not a simple one. I've been recording the intervals for twenty years. Most people don't stay long enough to see it as a pattern.
watching.how_long: Twenty-two years now. I came to the island to document the tidal migration charts and didn't leave. The patterns kept getting more interesting. I keep telling myself I'll finish the work and go. A long pause. The work isn't finished.

island.first: The harbor holds the island's oldest secrets. The village has records. The archive has records. The harbor has the sea, which remembers differently — in pressure and in pull. More honestly, I've always thought.

patterns.intro: The surfacing pattern has three registers. A main cycle — thirty-one days, consistent as a tide table. An exception cycle — seven days, irregular, weather-adjacent but not weather-driven. And one pattern I haven't categorised yet. Something new. Started within the last year.
patterns.detail: The main cycle is almost certainly Vael's biological rhythm — if biological is the right word for a bound entity. The creature surfaces to align with whatever process the lighthouse regulates. The exception days are different. On exception days, the surfacing is urgent. Not feeding. Something closer to alarm.

feeding.pattern: Not feeding — I want to be careful about that word. The creature doesn't surface to eat. It surfaces to witness. The moment the lighthouse beam sweeps the water, it rises. As if it needs confirmation that the light is still operating. Whether that's hope or terror, I've never been able to decide.

change.hint: Something changed last year. The exception days — they're multiplying. When I started, there were two per month, on average. Now there are five, sometimes six. The interval is compressing. Whatever the exception days signal, it's becoming more frequent.
change.detail: I don't know what to make of it yet. The data is clear — the rate is increasing. But I don't know what the exception days actually represent. I know what they look like from the surface: the creature surfaces and stays up for longer than usual, oriented east toward the lighthouse, and then submerges fast. As if it made a decision and acted on it.

exception.days: The exception days are the part I can't fully explain yet. They fall outside the main cycle. Not randomly — there's structure to them, but it's a structure I haven't cracked in twenty-two years. Something triggers them that I can't measure from the pier. Something that happens inside the lighthouse, I think. Or under it.
exception.increase: Six in the last thirty days. That's twice the rate from three years ago. Whatever is driving the exception days is accelerating. The lighthouse mechanism, maybe — something in its operation is changing. Or something in Vael is changing. Or both. The pier doesn't tell me which. It only tells me the frequency.

session1.open: I'll show you the first register. The main cycle. Twenty-two years of observations, reduced to intervals. Don't write it down yet — just listen. The numbers will mean more once you understand the shape of them. Ready?
session1.record: She speaks for a long time, tracing intervals in the air with one weathered finger. The pattern emerges slowly — a thirty-one-day pulse, consistent and unhurried, like the breathing of something enormous. That's the first layer. You've started to understand the rhythm. Come back. There's more.

session2.open: The exception register. What I know and what I suspect — I'll tell you both, clearly labelled. A scientist's honesty: the data ends where interpretation begins. She turns. Are you ready to hear where my certainty runs out?
session2.record: She speaks more carefully this time, pausing to distinguish fact from inference. The exception days emerge not as anomalies but as a response pattern — something happening inside the lighthouse triggers them within hours. The creature is reacting to something the lighthouse does, not to the sea. That's everything I can demonstrate. Come back for the last piece. That one I'll need your help with.

session3.open: The new pattern started fourteen months ago. I know the exact date because I remember being baffled by the first occurrence. Three surfacings within six hours, aligned with no cycle I'd documented. I logged it as anomaly. Then it happened again. And again. Now it happens every twelve to eighteen days without fail. She turns to you. I need you to help me record the rest. Not because my hands are failing — because this is the kind of knowledge that should have a witness.
session3.complete: When she finishes speaking, the harbor is very quiet. The numbers she's given you describe something like a countdown — not to a date, but to a threshold. The exception days will exceed the ordinary days before long. The pattern will invert. That's the full record. Everything I have. Twenty-two years of watching something that most people decided not to see. She closes the notebook. Now you know what I know.

scholar.map: She looks at your map for a long time before speaking. You've got the channel depths marked here. And the bearing variations. Someone taught you to look carefully. All right. I can compress what took me two months into two conversations, for someone who can read a chart. Show me what you have.
scholar.session1: She works quickly once she understands your fluency with cartography — tracing the pattern overlays directly onto your map, annotating intervals, marking the exception clusters. The thirty-one-day cycle resolves itself onto the paper with unexpected clarity. That's register one. Come back tomorrow. Register two is harder to map, but you'll manage.
scholar.complete: She finishes the second overlay and leans back. There it is. Everything I've spent twenty-two years watching, reduced to two pages of your chart. I'm not sure whether to be pleased or bereaved. She taps the paper lightly. The pattern is yours now. The knowledge travels with you.

pattern.sealed: I don't know what you'll do with it. I find I'm past caring about that — the knowledge should travel. I've kept it on this pier long enough. Whatever Vael is preparing for — whatever the inversion means — you'll understand it better than I do now. You've seen the lighthouse from the inside.
pattern.meaning: My best reading? The pattern is Vael counting. Not in time — in cycles. Each exception day marks a moment when the binding weakens fractionally. The new pattern marks when the weakening becomes cumulative. Something is happening to the mechanism that isn't supposed to happen. I've watched it from the sea for twenty-two years. You're the first Keeper who's actually asked about it. That tells me something about the other Keepers. I'm not sure what.

cal.name: Cal
cal.title: The Former Keeper's Silence

greeting.tier0: A figure stands at the cliff edge, facing the water. She does not turn when you approach. The wind takes whatever she might be saying before it reaches you.
greeting.tier1: You found it, then. The — yes. I have been waiting. Three loops, maybe more. ... You know what I am, or you would not be here.
greeting.tier2: There are things I have not said yet. You need the context before the conclusion. Before I give you — there is an order to this.
greeting.tier3: I have rehearsed this conversation for years. What I would say. How much I would give. ... Here we are. Ask me what you need to ask.
greeting.tier4: You are still here. I hoped you would come back. There are things I should have said before you left.

cal.identity: The keeper before the keeper before you. That is the — the simplest way to put it. I ran it, and then I did not. I was replaced. I left. Those two things happened in the same week. They were not the same choice.
cal.driven_away: You do not have enough. You are guessing at something you have not earned yet. Come back when you know what you are accusing me of. ... She is at the cliff edge, and then she is not.

handover.what_left: A sealed record. Third course of the foundation stones, north wall — I put it there the night before I left. I thought: someone will find this in a week. Someone will need it. ... That was three keepers ago.
handover.what_told: The council said the mechanism maintained the light. Structural support. Automated. They gave me a manual — forty pages of technical language, and three pages at the back I was not supposed to read. I read them. That was where it started.
handover.what_found: What they told me it did and what I watched it do were — ... The light called something. Something from the water. When the beam swept the bay at the right angle, there was movement. Not chance. A pattern. I have no name for what came up. I am not sure I ever did.
handover.left_behind: The original schematics. Before the modifications. I photographed every page. The photographs are in the sealed record. If you have the record, you have what I knew.

path.accept_truth: Then I will tell you everything. Not — not the version I have been telling myself. The actual sequence. From the beginning. ... It takes a long time. She speaks with the precision of someone who has been cutting every sentence down to its minimum for twenty years.
path.reject_truth: ... That is — yes. All right. You have decided the cost is too high. I understand that. I made the same calculation, once. I chose differently. I wonder, sometimes, which of us was right.
path.hidden_room: You have spoken with Dov. ... I can hear it — the way you are framing this. Yes. There is a room. Not on the surface — in the rock, below the path, behind the second overhang. I sealed it when I left. I will tell you how to open it.
path.schematic_revealed: The last schematics. The complete ones. He built something in that room that was not on any official record — the keeper before me. A failsafe. Or — I called it a failsafe, then. I think now it may have been a trap. The distinction matters if you are planning to use it.

aftermath.keeper_history: He knew what it cost. From the first loop — he could see further than the rest of us, forward and back. He chose to keep the light anyway. That is not — I have been trying to find the right word for it for twenty years. It might be that there is not one.
aftermath.dismantling: There is a sequence. Three components — you take the stabilisers first, both of them, before you touch the drive mechanism. If you take the drive first, it does not just stop. It completes. One final cycle. The stabilisers first. That part is not optional.

oren.name: Oren
oren.title: The Priest's Penance

greeting.tier0: This place does not receive visitors. Nor do I.
greeting.tier1: You have returned more than once. That suggests either persistence or a purpose you have not yet named. I am uncertain which I prefer.
greeting.tier2: You ask better questions than most who have come here. I performed a ritual in these ruins. I will say that much. What it accomplished — or failed to accomplish — is a longer answer.
greeting.tier3: I believe you have come weighted. Not with guilt, perhaps. With purpose. I have been waiting for someone who carries the weight correctly. I will tell you what I know.
greeting.tier4: The account is given. What remains is the work. If you require the words again, I will speak them.

why.here: I am here because I have not yet completed what I came to do. The work was begun. It was not finished. I stay until it is finished, or until I understand that it cannot be.

ruins.history: These were a chapel. Before that, a gathering place for older observances. Before that, something I have found no record of. Each age builds over what the previous age could not understand and called dangerous instead.
ruins.older: The older practice involved binding. Not sealing — those are different things. What is sealed may be opened. What is bound persists until someone with the knowledge and the will undoes it. The binding requires consent, in theory. In practice, this was not always observed.

oren.identity: I was a priest. Thirty years ago, perhaps forty. I came to this island to perform a service. I have not left because the service was not completed as it was intended. I remain. I catalogue. I wait.

service.evasion: A ritual. Sanctioned, I told myself, by the authority that sent me. I have had many years in which to consider whether that sanction was genuine. The conclusion I have reached is not comfortable.

ritual.admission: It was a binding. A practitioner’s binding — not metaphor, not symbol. The words, spoken correctly, constrain a soul. Prevent its passage. Hold it between what it was and what it should become. I believed, when I performed it, that I had been given consent. I am less certain now.
ritual.who_bound: That is the question that requires an answer I am not yet prepared to speak aloud. It has one. I have known it for thirty years. When I can say it without flinching — without making it a confession rather than a fact — I will say it.
ritual.persisted: Something that should have dissolved. Every binding, properly performed, releases at death — the spirit passes and the words lose their hold. This one did not release. The soul remained. Whether that is because I erred, or because the soul was unwilling, or because the words were wrong — I have been examining that question since the morning after.
ritual.wrong: Yes. Something persisted that should not have persisted. That is the short answer. The longer answer involves thirty years of notes and a great deal of uncertainty about what it means for a ritual to have gone wrong when the effect continues.
ritual.teaching: The words are few. Speak directly to what is bound — not about it, not around it. Address it as you would address a person wrongfully confined. Acknowledge its presence. Then: As you were before the word was spoken. So you are released. Say it as though you mean it. The ritual has no use for ceremony if the intent is absent.

confession.full: What I bound was not willing. I told myself it was. I had documentation — consent forms, signatures, a record sealed with the church's mark. I have examined those documents many times since. I do not believe the consent was genuine. A soul is not freed by the ritual's expiration. It is held, and it waits. What I performed here holds something that wished to move on. I am the reason it cannot.

path_a.argument: The inscription. You have read the inscription. Then you know what I had only suspected. The original binding — not mine, the one that predates me by a century — was also performed without genuine consent. I perpetuated a practice I had been told was consecrated. I see now it was merely old, and age had been mistaken for sanctity. You have given me something. I will give you what I have.

path_b.condemn: You may say so. I have not required absolution of strangers — I did not think I had earned the right to ask for it. I require it of myself, and I have not provided it. What you say may be true. I will sit with it. I ask only that you not return until I have considered whether your accounting is more precise than mine.

path_c.confess: Then hear the full account. There was a second ritual. Years after the first — unsanctioned, performed alone. A spirit was in distress. I believed I could ease it with a partial re-binding, one that would hold it more gently. I did not know how to complete the second ritual properly. It is still incomplete. That incompleteness is a wound in the original binding. The spirit you may have sensed is not at peace — it is caught between two sets of instructions, neither of which was correctly given. That is my error. The first and the second. I am the author of both.

absolved.ritual: The freeing words are these. Speak them to what is bound. As you were before the word was spoken. So you are released. Say it as if you mean it. If you do not mean it — if you speak the words as formula rather than as acknowledgment — the binding will not hear them. This is not metaphor. The binding recognizes intent.
absolved.vael_lore: Vael is not entirely bound in the way the others are. There is an interval — a specific frequency in the lighthouse beam — that Vael can receive as an act of will. If you activate the mechanism at the precise moment the bound soul’s distress is highest, the binding weakens briefly. That is the window. It lasts as long as the moment of genuine question. You will know the moment. It is the one that feels like there is no answer.

greeting.tier0: The air in the cave is wrong. Too still. The sound of water slows and then does not. Something is here that was not here before.
greeting.tier1: There is something in the corner. Not a shadow -- shadows do not wait. It turns and it has the shape of a child. It looks at you like it has been looking for a long time. You smell like the light, it says. I like that smell.
greeting.tier2: She is watching you when you come in. She does not pretend not to be watching. You came back, she says. I thought you might not. Most things do not come back.
greeting.tier3: She is sitting on the cave floor. Her feet do not quite touch it. She looks up when you enter. I have been thinking, she says. About what happens next. I think something is supposed to happen next. Do you know what it is?
greeting.tier4: She looks different. The cave looks different. Something has settled or something has shifted. You are not sure which.

first_contact.who: I am Sera. I was sent to get water. That was before. I do not know how long before. I still have not got the water.
first_contact.light_smell: The light on the hill. It smells warm. Before, the Keeper used to let me stand near the glass. I liked it. You have that smell on you. Did you come from there?
first_contact.how_long: I do not know how long. The sky starts over a lot. I counted for a while and then I stopped. Counting did not change anything.

history.before: Before is small. I had a dog. Its name was Patch because it had a patch. I had a bed that smelled like rosemary because my mother put sprigs in. I was going to fetch water. That is the last before thing I remember.
history.nothing_then: Then nothing. And then this. The cave. The sound of water. I thought I was still going to fetch it for a long time. Then I understood I was not going anywhere.
history.cave_forever: I do not know why here. Nobody told me. There is no one to ask. The fish do not talk. I tried.
history.sky_loops: The sky starts at the beginning and goes to the end and then starts again. I think it is the same sky. I think it is a different sky. I cannot tell. Is that strange? I used to think it was strange. Now I just think it is what the sky does.

choice.accept: She listens very carefully. Her face does not change. Okay, she says. I think I understand. She does not sound afraid. She sounds like someone who has been waiting a long time to be told something simple.
choice.resist: She goes very still. The water sound in the cave changes. The air gets colder. Why? she asks. The word is quiet and precise. Why would you do that.
choice.free: She stands up very slowly. Her eyes are wide but her mouth is a small, careful line. She says: I have been waiting. Not for the water. For someone who knew the words. Do you know them?

resolution.peace: Her outline softens at the edges. Thank you, she says. I understand now. I fell. The Keeper needed the piece and I went to get it and I fell and I did not finish the task. She pauses. I think that is all right. I think I am allowed to not finish now.
resolution.rage: The cave walls shudder. Small stones drop from the ceiling. She does not scream -- she just stands there, and the world goes wrong around her. I waited so long, she says. I was so patient. And you came and you knew and you chose to leave me here.
resolution.freed: She takes one step and then another and then she is not there. Not gone like sleep. Gone like a window opened. The air in the cave is different now. Emptier. Cleaner. The water sounds normal.
resolution.linger: She is still here, or the echo of her is. The shape of a child, watching the water. I am not sure what I am yet, she says. I am still deciding.

choice.ask_gear_kept: Ask about the gear kept back
choice.confront_betrayal: Confront her with what you know
choice.ask_break_binding: Ask how the binding could be undone
choice.ask_original_imprisonment: Ask who imprisoned them and why
choice.show_logbook: I found the old keeper's logbook...
choice.show_compass: This compass washed up near the dock.
choice.show_flare: I found a signal flare on the cliff.
choice.show_key: Does this rusted key mean anything to you?
choice.show_chart: I recovered a tide chart from the caves.
choice.tell_partial_truth: Tell him it controls the lighthouse mechanism
choice.offer_to_record: Offer to help record the patterns
choice.begin_second_session: Ask to continue the pattern work
choice.begin_final_session: Ask to complete the pattern record
choice.show_map: Show her your map of the harbor
choice.ask_how_long: How long have you worked here?
choice.ask_schematics: Tell me exactly what the schematics showed.
choice.ask_bram: Does Bram know any of this?
choice.ask_mechanism_purpose: What do you think it was truly for?
choice.ask_herbs: What do you know of herbs and remedies?
choice.ask_shadow_root: The shadow root — what is it, and why do you need it?
choice.ask_knowledge: How did you learn all of this?
choice.fetch_herb: I'll find the shadow root for you.
choice.negotiate_upfront: Give me the schematic first, then I'll fetch it.
choice.suggest_substitute: There may be a substitute herb.
choice.ask_what_doing: What are you doing out here?
choice.rudd_refuse_package: I want no part of this
choice.rudd_opened_package: I opened it. I needed to know.
choice.admit_curiosity: I was curious. I make no apology for it.
choice.deny_intent: I didn't mean to betray your trust
choice.press_mediate: There is another way through this
choice.accept_mediation: Agreed. I will arrange the meeting.
choice.ask_village_news: What is the mood in the village?
choice.ask_about_petra: Ask what you know about Petra
choice.ask_strangers: Have you seen any unusual visitors?
choice.ask_inn_history: Tell me about the inn's history
choice.ask_old_guests: Who stayed here in the old days?
choice.ask_real_problem: Tell me what is really going on
choice.ina_buyout_blackmail: I will settle the debt myself
choice.ina_mediate_resolution: I have spoken to Rudd — he will meet
choice.investigate_first: I will look into this before deciding
choice.ask_records_truth: Tell me what truly happened with the records
choice.ask_about_rudd: Ask about the man at the door
choice.accept_reward: I will take what you are offering
choice.reject_truth: The truth is not worth the cost
choice.ask_dismantling: Ask how to dismantle it safely
choice.ask_what_persisted: Ask what persisted after the ritual
choice.present_inscription: Present the inscription from the ruins
choice.offer_absolution: I don't condemn you for what you did
choice.condemn_him: You are responsible for the suffering here
choice.ask_ritual_words: Ask for the words of the ritual
choice.ask_light_smell: What do you mean -- smell like the light?
choice.ask_sky_loops: Does the sky keep starting over?
choice.sera_accept: There is peace to be found here. Let me help you find it.
choice.sera_resist: You do not have to accept this.
choice.sera_free: I know words that can release you.
choice.confirm_accept: You can rest now. You have waited long enough.
choice.confirm_resist: The binding was wrong. Rage against it.
choice.confirm_free: I speak the ritual. I ask for your release.
